content.text: Rotating Credentials

Credential rotation is the practice of periodically replacing secrets, certificates, or other authentication credentials before they expire or become compromised. For Azure service principals, rotation is the primary operational burden: managed identities and Workload Identity Federation eliminate this burden entirely by design. When credentials are unavoidable, rotation must be automated — manual rotation is error-prone and routinely missed.

attrs.alt: Credential rotation lifecycle: create new, deploy, switch traffic, revoke old

content.text: Why Rotation Matters

A credential that never changes becomes progressively more dangerous over time. Every additional day it exists increases the probability that it has been copied to a log file, accidentally committed to source control, or exfiltrated by a threat actor. Rotation limits the blast radius of any single credential compromise by bounding its useful lifetime.
A leaked client secret is usable until it expires or is revoked. A 2-year secret gives an attacker up to 2 years of access.
Certificates with long validity windows (5–10 years) are common in legacy systems and create the same risk.
Rotation also creates a natural audit checkpoint: if the new credential does not work, it surfaces broken integrations before they become silent outages.
Regulatory frameworks (SOC 2, PCI-DSS, ISO 27001) typically require secrets to be rotated at least annually; 90 days is the recommended baseline.

The best rotation policy is one that never needs to run — achieved by using managed identities or WIF instead of credentials. Reserve automated rotation for the cases where stored credentials genuinely cannot be avoided.
Key Vault as the Rotation Hub

Azure Key Vault is the standard place to store credentials that must be rotated. It provides versioned secrets, access policies, rotation policies with Event Grid notifications, and audit logs. Applications should always read secrets by name (without pinning a version), so that a rotation operation is transparent to the application.
Storing and reading a client secret

# Store the SP client secret in Key Vault
az keyvault secret set \
  --vault-name myKV \
  --name sp-client-secret \
  --value '$SECRET_VALUE' \
  --expires $(date -u -d '+90 days' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)

# Application reads the current version by name (no version suffix)
# The latest enabled version is always returned
SECRET=$(az keyvault secret show \
  --vault-name myKV \
  --name sp-client-secret \
  --query value -o tsv)

from azure.identity import ManagedIdentityCredential
from azure.keyvault.secrets import SecretClient

# Application uses MI to read Key Vault — never stores the secret itself
credential = ManagedIdentityCredential()
kv = SecretClient(vault_url='https://myKV.vault.azure.net', credential=credential)

# Read by name only — always gets the latest enabled version
client_secret = kv.get_secret('sp-client-secret').value

# Use the secret to authenticate a downstream SP
from azure.identity import ClientSecretCredential
sp_cred = ClientSecretCredential(
    tenant_id=TENANT_ID,
    client_id=CLIENT_ID,
    client_secret=client_secret
)
Automated Rotation with Key Vault Rotation Policies

Key Vault supports built-in rotation policies that automatically generate a new secret version at a defined interval. Combined with Event Grid notifications, applications can refresh their cached credential on a rotation event without any downtime.
Configure a rotation policy (CLI)

The built-in rotation only generates a new Key Vault secret version — it does not automatically create a new SP client secret in Entra ID. You must wire a Function App or Logic App to the rotation event to: (1) create the new SP credential, (2) store it in Key Vault, and (3) disable the old version.
Azure Function triggered by Key Vault rotation event

import azure.functions as func
import json, logging
from azure.identity import ManagedIdentityCredential
from azure.keyvault.secrets import SecretClient
from msgraph import GraphServiceClient
from msgraph.generated.applications.item.add_password.add_password_post_request_body import AddPasswordPostRequestBody
from msgraph.generated.models.password_credential import PasswordCredential
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone

app = func.FunctionApp()

@app.event_grid_trigger(arg_name='event')
async def rotate_sp_secret(event: func.EventGridEvent):
    data = event.get_json()
    secret_name = data['ObjectName']
    vault_url = data['VaultName']

    cred = ManagedIdentityCredential()
    kv = SecretClient(vault_url=f'https://{vault_url}.vault.azure.net', credential=cred)

    # Read current metadata to find APP_ID stored as a tag
    secret_props = kv.get_secret_properties(secret_name)
    app_id = secret_props.tags.get('app_id')
    if not app_id:
        logging.error('No app_id tag on secret %s', secret_name)
        return

    # Create a new credential on the App Registration via Graph API
    graph = GraphServiceClient(credentials=cred)
    body = AddPasswordPostRequestBody()
    body.password_credential = PasswordCredential(
        display_name='auto-rotated',
        end_date_time=datetime.now(timezone.utc) + timedelta(days=90)
    )
    new_cred = await graph.applications.by_application_id(app_id).add_password.post(body)
    new_secret_value = new_cred.secret_text

    # Store new value in Key Vault (creates a new version)
    kv.set_secret(secret_name, new_secret_value,
        tags={'app_id': app_id},
        expires_on=datetime.now(timezone.utc) + timedelta(days=90))

    logging.info('Rotated secret for app %s', app_id)
Zero-Downtime Rotation Pattern

Naive rotation — delete the old credential, create a new one — causes a brief window where the old secret is gone but the new one is not yet deployed. The correct pattern overlaps credentials: the new credential exists and works before the old one is revoked.
Create the new credential (v2) on the App Registration. The old credential (v1) still works.
Store v2 in Key Vault as a new version of the same secret. Applications that read by name will start picking up v2 on their next cache refresh.
Wait for all running application instances to refresh their cached secret (one cache TTL window, typically 5–15 minutes).
Verify that v2 is being used by checking Entra ID sign-in logs for the new keyId.
Revoke v1 on the App Registration and disable the old Key Vault version.

APP_ID='00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'

# Step 1: Create new credential (v2), capture keyId
NEW_CRED=$(az ad app credential reset \
  --id $APP_ID \
  --append \
  --display-name 'rotated-$(date +%Y%m%d)' \
  --years 0.25 \
  --query '{secret:password, keyId:keyId}' -o json)
NEW_SECRET=$(echo $NEW_CRED | jq -r .secret)
NEW_KEY_ID=$(echo $NEW_CRED | jq -r .keyId)

# Step 2: Store in Key Vault (new version; apps start using it on next read)
az keyvault secret set \
  --vault-name myKV \
  --name sp-client-secret \
  --value "$NEW_SECRET" \
  --tags app_id=$APP_ID key_id=$NEW_KEY_ID

# Step 3: Wait for propagation (let app instances refresh)
echo 'Wait 15 minutes for apps to pick up new secret, then verify in sign-in logs'

# Step 4: List current credentials to find the old key ID
az ad app credential list --id $APP_ID -o table

# Step 5: Revoke old credential by keyId
OLD_KEY_ID='xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx'
az ad app credential delete --id $APP_ID --key-id $OLD_KEY_ID
Rotating Certificates

Certificate rotation follows the same overlap pattern but has additional steps: generating the new certificate, registering it with the App Registration, updating the workload's certificate store, and revoking the old one.

APP_ID='00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'

# Generate new certificate
openssl req -x509 -newkey rsa:4096 -keyout new_key.pem -out new_cert.pem \
  -days 90 -nodes -subj '/CN=myapp-sp-rotated'

# Register new cert with App Registration (keep old cert active)
az ad app credential reset \
  --id $APP_ID \
  --cert @new_cert.pem \
  --append

# Store private key securely in Key Vault as a certificate object
# (combines cert + key in PFX format)
openssl pkcs12 -export -out new_bundle.pfx \
  -inkey new_key.pem -in new_cert.pem -passout pass:

az keyvault certificate import \
  --vault-name myKV \
  --name sp-cert \
  --file new_bundle.pfx

# Update workload to load the new cert from Key Vault, then revoke old cert
OLD_THUMBPRINT='AABBCC...'
az ad app credential delete \
  --id $APP_ID \
  --key-id $OLD_THUMBPRINT
Key Vault Certificates with auto-renewal

Key Vault Certificate objects (not Secret objects) support built-in auto-renewal against a configured CA (DigiCert or GlobalSign) or via CSR-based renewal. The workload reads the certificate from Key Vault by name; rotation is transparent.

# Create a Key Vault Certificate with auto-renewal policy
az keyvault certificate create \
  --vault-name myKV \
  --name sp-cert \
  --policy '{
    "issuerParameters": { "name": "Self" },
    "keyProperties": { "keySize": 4096, "kty": "RSA", "reuseKey": false },
    "lifetimeActions": [{ "action": { "actionType": "AutoRenew" }, "trigger": { "daysBeforeExpiry": 30 } }],
    "secretProperties": { "contentType": "application/x-pkcs12" },
    "x509CertificateProperties": { "subject": "CN=myapp-sp", "validityInMonths": 3 }
  }'

# Workload reads cert by name — auto-renewed version is transparent
az keyvault certificate download \
  --vault-name myKV \
  --name sp-cert \
  --file current_cert.pem
Rotation for Different Credential Types
Credential type
Rotation mechanism
Downtime risk
Automation complexity
Client secret
Overlap (create new → switch → revoke old)
Low (with overlap)
Medium (KV + Function App + Graph API)
Certificate (self-signed)
Overlap (register new cert → update workload → revoke old)
Low (with overlap)
Medium (KV Certificate + workload reload)
Certificate (CA-issued via KV)
Auto-renewal via KV Certificate policy
None
Low (KV manages it)
Managed identity (SAMI/UAMI)
N/A — platform-managed
None
None
Workload Identity Federation
Update federated credential if subject/issuer changes
None
None (no stored credential)
Monitoring and Alerting for Rotation
KQL: detect credentials nearing expiry

// Entra ID App Registration credentials expiring in < 30 days
// Run from Log Analytics linked to an Entra diagnostic setting
AuditLogs
| where OperationName == 'Update application'
| extend creds = todynamic(tostring(TargetResources[0].modifiedProperties))
| mvexpand creds
| where creds.displayName in ('KeyCredentials', 'PasswordCredentials')
| extend NewValue = todynamic(tostring(creds.newValue))
| mvexpand NewValue
| extend ExpiryDate = todatetime(NewValue.endDateTime)
| where ExpiryDate < ago(-1d) and ExpiryDate < now() + 30d
| project ExpiryDate, AppName=tostring(TargetResources[0].displayName),
           InitiatedBy=tostring(InitiatedBy.user.userPrincipalName)
| order by ExpiryDate asc
KQL: detect login with a secret after rotation (old keyId still working)

// Track which keyId is being used for SP logins
// If you see the old keyId after rotation, the app has not picked up the new secret
AADServicePrincipalSignInLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(24h)
| where AppDisplayName == 'myapp'
| project TimeGenerated, KeyId=ServicePrincipalCredentialKeyId,
           CredType=iff(ServicePrincipalCredentialKeyId != '', 'Cert/WIF', 'Secret'),
           IPAddress
| order by TimeGenerated desc
Common Rotation Mistakes
Pinning a specific Key Vault secret version in the application: version pinning means rotation never takes effect. Always read by name only.
Revoking the old credential before verifying the new one works: causes immediate outage. Always test the new credential in staging before revoking the old one.
Setting a 90-day expiry but triggering rotation at day 89: leaves no buffer for failures. Start rotation at 70-80% of lifetime (63-72 days for a 90-day credential).
Not tagging Key Vault secrets with metadata (app_id, key_id): the rotation Function App cannot determine which App Registration to update. Always tag secrets with the SP's app_id.
Rotating only the Key Vault secret without updating the App Registration: the secret in Key Vault is out of sync with what Entra ID accepts. Rotation must update both.
GitHub Actions Pipeline for Automated Rotation

CI/CD pipelines can drive rotation on a schedule: a workflow creates a new SP credential, updates Key Vault, waits for propagation, verifies via Entra sign-in logs, then revokes the old credential. This approach keeps the rotation logic in version control and produces an audit trail in the workflow run history.

name: Rotate SP Client Secret

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 3 * * 1'  # Every Monday at 03:00 UTC (adjust to ~70% of secret lifetime)
  workflow_dispatch:      # Allow manual trigger

permissions:
  id-token: write  # Required for OIDC login
  contents: read

jobs:
  rotate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Azure login (via WIF — no stored secret needed for the automation itself)
        uses: azure/login@v2
        with:
          client-id: ${{ secrets.ROTATION_SP_CLIENT_ID }}
          tenant-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
          subscription-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}

      - name: Create new SP credential
        id: create_cred
        run: |
          NEW_CRED=$(az ad app credential reset \
            --id ${{ secrets.TARGET_APP_ID }} \
            --append \
            --display-name "rotated-$(date +%Y%m%d)" \
            --years 0.25 \
            --query '{secret:password, keyId:keyId}' -o json)
          echo "NEW_KEY_ID=$(echo $NEW_CRED | jq -r .keyId)" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          # Store secret securely — never echo it
          echo "::add-mask::$(echo $NEW_CRED | jq -r .secret)"
          az keyvault secret set \
            --vault-name ${{ secrets.KV_NAME }} \
            --name sp-client-secret \
            --value "$(echo $NEW_CRED | jq -r .secret)" \
            --tags app_id=${{ secrets.TARGET_APP_ID }} key_id=$(echo $NEW_CRED | jq -r .keyId)

      - name: Wait for apps to pick up new secret
        run: sleep 300  # 5 min cache window

      - name: Verify new keyId appears in sign-in logs
        run: |
          NEW_KEY_ID='${{ steps.create_cred.outputs.NEW_KEY_ID }}'
          SEEN=$(az monitor log-analytics query \
            --workspace ${{ secrets.LOG_ANALYTICS_WORKSPACE_ID }} \
            --analytics-query "AADServicePrincipalSignInLogs | where TimeGenerated > ago(10m) and ServicePrincipalCredentialKeyId == '$NEW_KEY_ID' | count" \
            --query '[0].Count' -o tsv)
          if [ "$SEEN" -gt '0' ]; then
            echo 'New credential verified in sign-in logs'
          else
            echo 'WARNING: new credential not yet seen — check application logs'
            exit 1
          fi

      - name: Revoke old credentials (keep only latest)
        run: |
          # List all credentials and delete those not created today
          TODAY=$(date +%Y%m%d)
          az ad app credential list --id ${{ secrets.TARGET_APP_ID }} -o json | \
            jq -r '.[] | select(.displayName | startswith("rotated-") | not or (.displayName | ltrimstr("rotated-")) < "'$TODAY'") | .keyId' | \
            while read KID; do
              az ad app credential delete --id ${{ secrets.TARGET_APP_ID }} --key-id $KID
              echo "Revoked old credential: $KID"
            done

The rotation automation SP itself uses WIF (OIDC from GitHub Actions) — it has no stored credential. This is the key insight: use WIF to authenticate the rotation pipeline, and only manage stored credentials for the target SPs that cannot yet be migrated to WIF.
Terraform: Managing SP Credentials with Rotation-Aware HCL

Terraform's `azuread_application_password` resource manages SP client secrets as IaC. The `rotate_when_changed` meta-argument triggers rotation when a referenced timestamp changes — for example, a variable updated in the pipeline on each rotation run.

On each rotation cycle, the pipeline updates `rotation_timestamp` (e.g., to the current date string) and runs `terraform apply`. Terraform destroys the old password resource and creates a new one; the Key Vault secret is updated atomically in the same plan.
Emergency Revocation

When a credential is suspected to be compromised, rotation is not enough — the old credential must be revoked immediately, not at the end of the overlap window. This requires accepting a brief outage until applications pick up the replacement.

# Emergency: revoke ALL credentials on an App Registration immediately
APP_ID='00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'

# List all credentials
az ad app credential list --id $APP_ID -o table

# Revoke a specific credential by keyId
COMPROMISED_KEY_ID='xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx'
az ad app credential delete --id $APP_ID --key-id $COMPROMISED_KEY_ID

# Immediately create a new credential and push to Key Vault
NEW_SECRET=$(az ad app credential reset \
  --id $APP_ID \
  --query password -o tsv)

az keyvault secret set \
  --vault-name myKV \
  --name sp-client-secret \
  --value "$NEW_SECRET"

# Force app restart to pick up new secret immediately (App Service example)
az webapp restart --name myApp --resource-group myRG
After emergency revocation, investigate the root cause: how was the credential accessed? Check Entra ID sign-in logs for the compromised keyId.
File an incident report and review whether the compromised SP had excessive permissions (Contributor, Owner) that allowed lateral movement.
Consider migrating the SP to WIF or a managed identity after the incident to eliminate the credential entirely.
Summary

Credential rotation reduces the window of exposure for any compromised secret or certificate. The key principle is overlap: the new credential must be live and verified before the old one is revoked. Key Vault centralises rotation logic, provides version history, and integrates with Event Grid to notify applications. For client secrets, a Function App handles the Entra ID side of rotation; for certificates, Key Vault's built-in auto-renewal can make the process fully automatic. The best outcome is to eliminate credentials entirely — managed identities and Workload Identity Federation should be the first choice for any new workload.
